questions.q: On the cotangent bundle $T^*Q$ with canonical coordinates $(q^i,p_i)$, the tautological 1-form is $\theta=\sum p_i\,dq^i$. The canonical symplectic form is then:
questions.hint: $\omega$ is by definition the exterior derivative of the tautological 1-form (up to sign convention).
questions.explain: By construction $\omega = -d\theta = \sum dq^i\wedge dp_i = \sum dp_i\wedge dq^i$ depending on sign convention. Either sign gives a closed non-degenerate 2-form. Note $\theta\wedge\theta=0$ since $\theta$ is a 1-form.

questions.q: If $\dim Q = n$, what is $\dim T^*Q$, and why is this the right dimension for phase space?
choices: $2n$ ŌĆö a state needs both position and conjugate momentum.
$n+1$ ŌĆö one extra dimension for time.
questions.hint: Newton's second law is second-order in $q$; converting to first order doubles the variables.
questions.explain: Each fibre $T^*_qQ$ has dimension $n$, so $T^*Q$ has dimension $2n$. This matches the phase-space dimension demanded by mechanics: a second-order ODE in $q$ becomes a first-order system in $(q,p)$, which is what Hamilton's equations realise on $T^*Q$.

questions.q: Why are the canonical coordinates $(q^i,p_i)$ on $T^*Q$ automatically Darboux coordinates?
choices: Because $\omega = \sum dp_i\wedge dq^i$ already has the Darboux normal form in those coordinates.
Because every chart is a Darboux chart.
questions.hint: Darboux's theorem promises such coordinates exist; on $T^*Q$ they're already built in.
questions.explain: Darboux's theorem says any symplectic manifold has local coordinates in which $\omega = \sum dp_i\wedge dq^i$. On $T^*Q$ the construction $\omega = -d\theta$ produces this form directly in the natural fibred coordinates ŌĆö no further straightening needed.

questions.q: For the simple harmonic oscillator $H = \tfrac{p^2}{2m} + \tfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2 q^2$, Hamilton's equations are:
questions.hint: $\dot q = \partial H/\partial p,\ \dot p = -\partial H/\partial q$.
questions.explain: Differentiating: $\partial H/\partial p = p/m$ so $\dot q = p/m$. And $\partial H/\partial q = m\omega^2 q$, so $\dot p = -m\omega^2 q$. Eliminating $p$ recovers $\ddot q + \omega^2 q = 0$.

questions.q: Why is the Hamiltonian $H$ automatically conserved along trajectories of $X_H$?
choices: It's not ŌĆö $H$ can change in time.
questions.hint: Apply the defining identity $\iota_{X_H}\omega = dH$ to $X_H$ itself.
questions.explain: By definition $\iota_{X_H}\omega = dH$, so $dH(X_H) = \omega(X_H,X_H) = 0$ since $\omega$ is alternating. Hence $H$ is constant along the flow ŌĆö energy conservation is geometric, not a separate axiom.

questions.q: Liouville's theorem says the Hamiltonian flow preserves which structure?
choices: The number of trajectories passing through a point.
The Hamiltonian's value at the origin.
questions.hint: Phase-space volume is the top exterior power of $\omega$.
questions.explain: $\mathcal{L}_{X_H}\omega = d(\iota_{X_H}\omega) + \iota_{X_H}d\omega = d(dH) + 0 = 0$. So $X_H$ preserves $\omega$, hence its top power $\omega^n/n!$ ŌĆö the phase-space volume. Distances and trajectory counts are not preserved in general.

questions.q: A diffeomorphism $\varphi\colon T^*Q\to T^*Q$ is called canonical (symplectic) when:
questions.hint: The defining condition is preservation of the symplectic form, not of any particular Hamiltonian.
questions.explain: Canonical/symplectic means $\varphi^*\omega=\omega$. This implies that Hamilton's equations transform covariantly: if $\varphi^*\omega=\omega$ then the flow of $X_H$ pulls back to the flow of $X_{H\circ\varphi}$. Linearity is unnecessary; preserving a specific $H$ is a stronger condition.

questions.q: A type-1 generating function $S(q,Q)$ defines a canonical transformation by which formulas?
questions.hint: Old momentum is the partial of $S$ with respect to old position; new momentum is the negative partial in the new position.
questions.explain: The condition $p\,dq - P\,dQ = dS$ on a Lagrangian graph in $T^*(Q\times Q)$ unpacks to $p = \partial S/\partial q,\ P = -\partial S/\partial Q$. Different sign conventions exist; this is the standard one.

questions.q: The HamiltonŌĆōJacobi equation $\partial S/\partial t + H(q,\partial S/\partial q,t) = 0$ comes from looking for a generating function $S$ such that:
choices: the new Hamiltonian $K$ vanishes identically, so the new coordinates are constants of motion.
questions.explain: Choosing $S$ so the transformed Hamiltonian $K = H + \partial S/\partial t$ vanishes makes the new coordinates $(Q,P)$ constants of motion: integrating Hamilton's equations is reduced to solving the (single, nonlinear, first-order) HamiltonŌĆōJacobi PDE.

questions.q: If $f\in C^\infty(T^*Q)$ satisfies $\{f,H\}=0$, then along the flow of $X_H$:
questions.explain: Along the Hamiltonian flow, $\dot f = X_H(f) = \omega(X_f,X_H) = \{f,H\}$. So $\{f,H\}=0$ exactly means $f$ is a first integral. This is Noether in Poisson form: symmetries (functions Poisson-commuting with $H$) are conserved quantities.

questions.q: For a free particle in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with $H = |p|^2/(2m)$, which symmetry corresponds to conservation of linear momentum $p_i$?
questions.explain: The flow of $X_{p_i}$ is the translation $q^i \mapsto q^i + t$, leaving all $p_j$ and $|p|^2$ unchanged. Since $\{p_i,H\} = 0$, the translation is a symmetry and $p_i$ is conserved. Time translation gives energy conservation; rotations give angular momentum.

questions.q: Which conserved quantity does the rotational symmetry $q\mapsto Rq,\ p\mapsto Rp$ ($R\in SO(3)$) of a central-potential Hamiltonian $H = |p|^2/(2m) + V(|q|)$ produce?
questions.hint: Rotations are generated by angular momentum components.
questions.explain: Each component $L_i$ generates rotations about axis $i$ via the Poisson bracket. For a rotationally symmetric $H$, $\{L_i,H\}=0$, so $L=q\times p$ is conserved. The LRL vector is the additional Kepler-specific symmetry beyond rotations.

questions.q: A Hamiltonian system on a $2n$-dimensional symplectic manifold is Liouville integrable when there exist:
choices: $2n$ constants of motion of any kind.
$n$ functionally independent first integrals $f_1=H,f_2,\dots,f_n$ that pairwise Poisson-commute.
questions.explain: LiouvilleŌĆōArnold requires $n$ functionally independent first integrals in involution ($\{f_i,f_j\}=0$). On a regular compact common level set, the joint flows give a free $\mathbb{R}^n$ action whose orbits are tori $T^n$; action-angle coordinates linearise the dynamics on each torus.

questions.q: In action-angle coordinates $(I,\theta)$, an integrable Hamiltonian becomes $H=H(I)$ alone. Hamilton's equations then read:
choices: $\dot I = 0,\ \dot\theta = \partial H/\partial I$ ŌĆö actions are constant, angles wind at frequencies $\omega(I)$.
questions.explain: $\dot I_i = -\partial H/\partial\theta_i = 0$ and $\dot\theta_i = \partial H/\partial I_i = \omega_i(I)$. Each invariant torus carries a linear flow with frequency vector $\omega(I)$; the dynamics is quasi-periodic.

questions.q: The KAM theorem (KolmogorovŌĆōArnoldŌĆōMoser) says that under a small non-degenerate perturbation $H_\varepsilon = H_0(I) + \varepsilon H_1(I,\theta)$ of an integrable $H_0$:
choices: All invariant tori are destroyed for any $\varepsilon>0$.
Most invariant tori survive ŌĆö those with sufficiently Diophantine (badly approximable) frequency vectors.
Only the tori with rational frequencies survive.
questions.hint: Resonant (rational-frequency) tori are exactly the ones that break.
questions.explain: KAM: tori whose frequency vectors $\omega(I)$ satisfy a Diophantine condition $|k\cdot\omega| \geq \gamma|k|^{-\tau}$ persist as smooth deformations for small $\varepsilon$. The rationally resonant tori shatter into Birkhoff zones with thin chaotic layers ŌĆö visible in numerical experiments as the gradual onset of disorder.

questions.q: The 1D harmonic oscillator $H = \tfrac{p^2}{2m} + \tfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2 q^2$ has phase portrait consisting of:
choices: Concentric ellipses (level sets of $H$) traversed at angular frequency $\omega$.
questions.hint: Level sets of a positive-definite quadratic form are ellipses.
questions.explain: Since $H$ is a positive-definite quadratic form in $(q,p)$, each level set $\{H=E\}$ is an ellipse. The flow rotates at angular frequency $\omega$ in the appropriately scaled coordinates $(q,p/m\omega)$ ŌĆö the iconic phase portrait of the harmonic oscillator.

questions.q: What makes the Kepler problem $H = |p|^2/(2m) - k/|q|$ super-integrable?
choices: Beyond energy and angular momentum, the LaplaceŌĆōRungeŌĆōLenz vector $A = p\times L - mk\hat q$ is also conserved, giving more independent integrals than dimensions of phase space allow generically.
It has only one degree of freedom.
questions.hint: Closed orbits are a strong constraint ŌĆö only the harmonic oscillator and the Kepler/Coulomb potential have all bounded orbits closed (Bertrand's theorem).
questions.explain: Generic integrable systems on a $2n$-manifold have $n$ commuting integrals. Kepler has $n+1$: $H$, $L = q\times p$, and the LRL vector $A$. The extra symmetry is hidden $SO(4)$ (for bound states), and it's why Kepler orbits close into ellipses rather than precessing.

questions.q: Geodesic flow on a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ is the Hamiltonian flow on $T^*M$ for which Hamiltonian?
choices: $H(q,p) = \tfrac{1}{2} g^{ij}(q) p_i p_j$, the dual metric on cotangent fibres.
questions.explain: Take the Riemannian metric $g_{ij}$, raise indices to $g^{ij}$, and form $H = \tfrac{1}{2} g^{ij}p_ip_j$. Hamilton's equations reproduce the geodesic equation; trajectories project to geodesics on $M$. Geometric mechanics often starts here.
